trade.summary: Spain and Mexico have significant bilateral trade supported by cultural and linguistic ties. Spain exports machinery, vehicles, pharmaceuticals, and food products; Mexico exports crude oil, vehicles, electronics, and agricultural products. The EU-Mexico modernized Global Agreement, once ratified, will boost trade further.

diplomatic.status: Special relationship with periodic political friction
diplomatic.summary: Spain and Mexico have the closest bilateral relationship of any European-Latin American pair due to shared history, language, and culture. However, relations have been periodically strained under Mexico's AMLO and Claudia Sheinbaum governments, including diplomatic spats over Spain's colonial history acknowledgments. Despite political friction, the relationship is deep-rooted and substantive. Spain's King and PM have faced direct Mexican government criticism.
recent_incidents: Diplomatic tensions over Spanish monarchy and colonial history statements (2019-2024)
Mexico's cooling of relations under AMLO

military.summary: Very limited military cooperation. No alliance. Mexico maintains a non-interventionist foreign policy. Spain provides some training and capacity building. The relationship is primarily economic and cultural.

financial.summary: Spain is Mexico's largest European investor and one of its largest foreign investors overall. Spanish firms (BBVA — Mexico's largest bank, Santander, Telefonica, Repsol, Iberdrola, ACS) have massive Mexican operations. Mexico is essential to the international earnings of most major Spanish companies.

energy.summary: Spanish energy firms (Iberdrola, Repsol, Naturgy) are major investors in Mexican energy. Iberdrola has been in dispute with the Mexican government over energy policy nationalization under AMLO. Spain imports some Mexican crude. Spanish renewable firms are positioned for Mexico's energy transition.

scientific.summary: Strong scientific and academic ties. Spain and Mexico share the Spanish-language academic community. Extensive researcher and student exchange. Joint research programs in archaeology, history, life sciences, and climate. Spain and Mexico co-produce a significant body of Spanish-language scientific literature.
